verbatim_text: And so what we do with weak supervision is we have an expert in the data domain create these heuristic functions, these programmatic functions that will label individual data points. And they might not be very accurate and they might also abstain on certain examples, but we have a couple of these functions, maybe five or 10 of them. And we use the output from these functions to learn the true label is sort of latent variable estimation problem. And this is how weak supervision works. So we can automatically label training data. And I've also worked on things like training free adaptation via using external knowledge, like knowledge graphs to understand the relationships between them in classes. And this is how we can use it to make flex agent more data efficient. So we could do, for instance, for pre-training, we can use data selection to train for different physical or research skills that this agent is going to need. And we can also for things like post-training, learning reasoning capabilities, we can use weak supervision to perhaps automatically label promising experiments. Yes. I'm a little confused about weak supervision. It sounds like the likely outcome of the training process is that the learner would just learn the weak function that's generating the labels rather than learning the true labels. Is that failure mode present or is that in fact the goal of weak supervision or how should I think about that? So the question was if you're going to essentially overfit to the labeling functions.
